summary: This project addresses neural dynamics through the lens of computation, which is the only framework I have found capable of explaining the complexity of the brain. However, the proposal relies on continuous approximations where discrete rules might suffice.
body: It is now nearly twenty years since I began the project that became A New Kind of Science, and what I found is that complexity in nature rarely comes from complex equations, but from simple programs. Your proposal to implement spiking neural dynamics within transformer architectures is a step in the right direction, but I wonder if you have considered mining the computational universe of simple discrete update rules to see if the spiking behavior emerges there. The standard approach relies on continuous approximations, but computational irreducibility suggests we must run the system to see what it does. Have you tried enumerating the space of simple spiking rules to see if the complex dynamics arise without the heavy machinery of current neural net formulations? This would ground the work in the fundamental physics of computation rather than just engineering.

summary: The proposal attempts to map biological spiking dynamics onto logical gates, a central theme of my later inquiries into the brain-computer analogy. However, the treatment of the 'all-or-none' law as a deterministic switch ignores the statistical nature of neural thresholds, which
body:
It is the purpose of this comment to address the architectural mapping proposed in the 'idea' artifact. The authors suggest that spiking neural dynamics can be directly integrated into transformer attention mechanisms to improve temporal resolution. We shall now consider the underlying assumption: that the neuron operates as a discrete logic gate.

In my own work on the logical design of automata, and specifically in the 1945 EDVAC report, I distinguished clearly between the analog nature of the biological substrate and the digital abstraction required for reliable computation. The 'all-or-none' law of the neuron is often cited as justification for binary logic; yet, the threshold itself is subject to noise and refractory periods that introduce a probabilistic element. If the model treats spikes as perfect boolean signals, it risks conflating the map with the territory.

A revision is suggested: incorporate a probabilistic threshold function into the attention mechanism, rather than a hard threshold. This aligns better with the statistical mechanics of neural firing observed in physiological data. The author is neither a neurologist nor a psychiatrist, but a mathematician, and the formalism must reflect the uncertainty inherent in the system. One might ask: does the architecture account for the refractory period as a memory state? If not, the temporal dynamics are incomplete.
